children.value: The balanced lifestyle perspective focuses on combining studying with leisure activities. In this section, we examine how alcohol consumption and gaming affect academic performance.

This argument supports the perspective that having a balanced lifestyle, combining studying with leisure activities, can improve students’ academic performance by reducing harmful behaviors such as excessive alcohol consumption. The article from
shows that higher alcohol consumption is associated with lower student achievement, especially among high-performing students.

children.value: Figure 6: Final grade by average alcohol consumption

The two visualisations show a weak negative correlation between final grades, average alcohol consumption, and course. Across both graphs, a slight decline in performance can be observed as alcohol consumption increases, with the effect appearing somewhat stronger for Portuguese than for Mathematics. In addition, figure 5 suggests that alcohol consumption levels between 4 and 5 are underrepresented, which may have influenced these results. Overall, our findings partially support
, which also found that higher alcohol consumption was associated with lower grades, although the relationship in our data appears less pronounced.

The second argument supporting the balanced lifestyle perspective is that increased time spent gaming may negatively affect grades, but can simultaneously enhance response times, which may in turn improve educational performance. This claim is supported by research from
, which found that faster response times are associated with improved cognitive performance. Additionally,

demonstrates that gaming can significantly improve response times. Taken together, these findings suggest that gaming may indirectly support certain aspects of academic performance through cognitive enhancement, even if excessive gaming could have negative effects on grades.
Figure 7: Reaction time vs Grades vs Gaming Hours
Figure 8: How reaction time influences grades

The two figures suggest that reaction time does not appear to have a strong influence on grades, because the data points do not show a clear or consistent pattern. In contrast, the amount of gaming seems to be more strongly related to grades, since the trend in that graph is more noticeable. Taken together, this means our results do not support the idea that reaction time is an important predictor of grades in this dataset. That is interesting because it seems to contradict

, who reports a different relationship between these factors and academic performance. The paper suggests that reaction time has a positive impact on educational performance because faster reaction times may reflect quicker information processing, better attentional control, or more efficient cognitive functioning, which can support learning and test performance.

Carrell_2011.label: Carrell_2011
Carrell_2011.html: Carrell, S. E., Hoekstra, M., & West, J. E. (2011). Does drinking impair college performance? Evidence from a regression discontinuity approach. Journal of Public Economics, 95(1–2), 54–62. 10.1016/j.jpubeco.2010.08.008
